2025 · ACL 2025 · Benchmarks & Evals

BIG-Bench Extra Hard

BIG-Bench Hard saturated — state-of-the-art models were clearing 90%+ on many of its 23 tasks. BBEH replaces every one of them with a harder analogue that demands more reasoning hops, longer inputs, and fewer shortcuts. The best general-purpose model manages 9.8% harmonic-mean accuracy; the best reasoning-specialized model, 44.8%.

Kazemi, Fatemi, Bansal, Palowitch, Anastasiou, Mehta, Jain, Aglietti, Jindal, Chen, Dikkala, Tyen, Liu, Shalit, Chiappa, Olszewska, Tay, Tran, Le, Firat (Google DeepMind, Google Research, UCLA)
venue ACL 2025 citations 110 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

For years, BIG-Bench Hard (BBH) — a 23-task subset of the original BIG-Bench (Srivastava et al., 2022) curated by Suzgun et al. (2022) — served as the community's default check on general LLM reasoning, precisely because it wasn't narrowly about math or code: it spanned temporal reasoning, spatial understanding, causal judgment, humor, logical deduction, and more. But the authors identify five concrete cracks that had opened up in BBH by 2025. First, a random-chance baseline already scores well because so many tasks have small answer spaces — 8 of the 23 tasks are binary, another 5 offer at most 5 options. Second, several tasks admit outright shortcuts: in the Geometric Shapes task, for instance, three "L" commands in the input reliably mean the answer is "triangle," with no actual shape-reasoning required. Third, BBH's inputs are short — a macro-average of about 700 characters per problem — far below what real-world reasoning tasks demand. Fourth, because the tasks were built to challenge older-generation models, most require only a few hops of reasoning. Fifth, even where BBH's skill coverage was good, it could be broadened further. The cumulative effect: state-of-the-art models were clearing 90%+ accuracy on many BBH tasks, and the benchmark had quietly dropped out of frontier model reports as a discriminating signal.

The fix on offer isn't a new benchmark from scratch — it's a like-for-like reconstruction. BBEH keeps BBH's 23-task skeleton (so the "vast array of reasoning skills" that made BBH useful in the first place is preserved) but swaps in a harder task for each slot: one that probes a similar reasoning capability, but resists the specific failure modes above.

// how it works

The method

Each of BBH's 23 tasks is replaced with a new one built to test the same broad skill family — but pushed further. BoardgameQA (based on Kazemi et al., 2023) replaces Logical Deduction, adding much greater reasoning depth (6–8 hops) and requiring the model to learn a conflict-resolution rule on the fly, rather than BBH's few-step deductions. Buggy Tables replaces Penguins-in-a-Table: instead of small, clean tables, models must reconstruct a large corrupted table from a description of exactly how it was corrupted, then answer conditional aggregate queries over it. Object Counting and Shuffled Objects get dramatically longer contexts and more distractor objects. Word Sorting adds a scrambled alphabet (r and p swapped) specifically to defeat models that fall back on memorized alphabetical order rather than actually applying the stated rule. Several tasks are adapted wholesale from other published benchmarks — Linguini (Sánchez et al., 2024) for linguistic reasoning, SportQA (Xia et al., 2024) for knowledge-intensive sports reasoning, a SpatialLLMEval-derived (Yamada et al., 2023) task for spatial reasoning, and causal-judgment items drawn from MoCa (Nie et al., 2023) and Kıcıman et al. (2023).

The construction process is explicitly semi-adversarial: the authors picked two reference models — Gemini 1.5 Flash for the general-purpose ceiling and Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking-Exp (the December 2024 build, later the January 2025 build) for the reasoning-specialized ceiling — and iterated on each task's difficulty until both reference models scored below 70% accuracy. When a task proved too easy, they either replaced it or added new sources of difficulty. This wasn't always straightforward: the paper's own worked example is the Boolean Expressions task, where simply adding more clauses failed to raise difficulty because the reference model was quietly generating a line of Python (result = <expression>; print(result)) to evaluate the expression directly. The fix was to replace some "True"/"False" literals with textual statements that evaluate to the same truth value (e.g., "The capital of Canada is Ottawa"), which blocks the naive code-execution shortcut. Twenty-three tasks means 200 questions each, except Disambiguation QA (120); a 460-example "BBEH Mini" subset (20 per task) is also released for cheaper iteration, scored by micro-average instead of harmonic mean given its smaller per-task counts.

For scoring, the paper uses the (adjusted) harmonic mean across the 23 task accuracies as its primary metric — deliberately chosen over the micro/macro averages typically used for multi-task benchmarks, because those can be dominated by a model excelling on a handful of tasks while collapsing on others. A value of 1 is added to every accuracy before taking the harmonic mean, purely to avoid division-by-zero on tasks where a model scores exactly 0%.

HM = nΣᵢ 1 / (accᵢ + 1) // harmonic mean over the n=23 task accuracies, +1-smoothed to handle zero scores — punishes spiky, uneven performance far more than a plain average would
// what they found

The results

Across 12 evaluated models (plus a random baseline) — spanning Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, Gemma2/Gemma3, Gemini 2.0 Flash and Flash-Lite, GPT-4o, DeepSeek R1 and its Qwen-32B distillation, and o3-mini (high) — the headline numbers land exactly where the abstract promises: 9.8% harmonic-mean accuracy for the best general-purpose model (Gemini 2.0 Flash) and 44.8% for the best reasoning-specialized model (o3-mini, high). The random baseline sits at 2.4% harmonic mean (8.4% micro average) — well below BBH's inflated random floor, confirming the small-answer-space problem was fixed. Under the (less punishing) micro-average metric, the ceilings rise to 23.9% (general-purpose) and 54.2% (reasoning), still leaving enormous headroom. Even the calibration model itself — Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking-Exp, one of the two references the benchmark was tuned against — only reaches 20.2% harmonic-mean accuracy, evidence the semi-adversarial tuning loop produced a genuinely hard benchmark rather than one secretly gamed for its own reference models. Difficulty carries over to non-reference models too: o3-mini (high) clears 70% accuracy on only 4 of the 23 tasks, DeepSeek R1 on just 3, and every other evaluated model never clears 70% on any single task.

Breaking performance down by task reveals where the reasoning-vs-general gap is largest. Comparing o3-mini (high) against GPT-4o, the largest absolute gains for the reasoning model cluster on counting, planning, arithmetic, and data-structure/algorithm tasks — e.g., Object Counting (GPT-4o: 6.5% → o3-mini: 90.0%) and Temporal Sequences (0.0% → 68.5%). The smallest gains — sometimes negative — cluster on commonsense, humor, sarcasm, and causal-judgment tasks such as NYCC (New Yorker Cartoon Caption) and SARC Triples, where GPT-4o is competitive with or ahead of the reasoning-specialized model. The same pattern, though weaker, shows up comparing model scale (Gemini 2.0 Flash vs. Flash-Lite): many-hop and algorithmic tasks gain the most from extra scale, humor/commonsense/causal tasks gain the least. Plotting these gains against each task's average context length and average output length (a proxy for "how much thinking the task demands") shows o3-mini's edge over GPT-4o widening as both increase — reasoning-style test-time compute pays off more on long, thinking-heavy problems.

Model classHarmonic mean (%)Micro avg (%)
Random baseline2.48.4
Best general-purpose (Gemini 2.0 Flash)9.823.9
Reference Thinking model (Gemini-2.0-Flash-Thinking-Exp)20.2—
Best reasoning-specialized (o3-mini, high)44.854.2
// does it hold up?

The eval read

BBEH is unusually candid for a benchmark paper — the authors devote a paragraph to flagging their own construction bias, which is rare and worth crediting. But the candor doesn't erase the underlying methodological wrinkle: the benchmark's difficulty was hand-tuned against exactly two Gemini-family models from the same lab that built it. The paper states plainly that "the choice of the reference model will unavoidably bias the benchmark towards certain types of failure modes" and that, because construction was adversarial with respect to the reference models, "a fair comparison of the reference and non-reference models may not be possible." The Boolean Expressions anecdote makes this concrete: the anti-shortcut patch exists specifically because one particular model tried solving the task via generated Python — a different reference model with different tool-use habits might never have triggered that fix, and the resulting task difficulty profile would look different.

There's also a model-coverage gap that stands out from a 2026 vantage point: no Claude model appears anywhere in the 12-model comparison, and neither does full o1, o3, or Gemini 2.5 — all released within a few months of this paper and squarely in its intended comparison class. For a benchmark whose stated purpose is comparing "reasoning-specialized" against "general-purpose" models, leaving out one of the three leading closed-model families entirely is a real hole, likely a cost/scale artifact of running 23 tasks × 200 questions across so many models rather than a deliberate exclusion, but a hole nonetheless.

baselines

Reasonably broad for its Feb 2025 vintage — 9 general-purpose models plus 3 reasoning models spanning Llama, Qwen, Gemma2/3, Gemini, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek R1 — but zero Claude models and no o1/o3/Gemini-2.5 leaves a real gap in the reasoning-model column specifically.

contamination

Roughly half the 23 tasks repackage already-public source datasets (BoardgameQA, MoCa/Kıcıman causal items, Linguini, SportQA, SpatialLLMEval, Test of Time) that can leak into future pretraining corpora independent of BBEH's own release; the fully procedural tasks (Buggy Tables, Word Sorting, Dyck Language, Multistep Arithmetic) are more contamination-resistant since fresh instances can be regenerated on demand.

what's missed

The harmonic mean is explicitly designed to punish spiky performance, but that also means a single near-zero task (Buggy Tables sits at 0.0–3.5% for every general-purpose model, and even DeepSeek R1 only reaches 4.5%) can dominate a model's aggregate score and obscure genuine strength elsewhere — a model excellent on 20 of 23 skills and merely weak on one still lands near the harmonic-mean floor.

would raise confidence

An ablation repeating the calibration loop with a non-Gemini anchor model (e.g., an o1/Claude pair) to show how much of the observed model rankings are an artifact of the two specific reference models used; evaluation of Claude and Gemini 2.5/o1 family models; and per-task pass/fail reporting alongside the harmonic-mean aggregate so a genuinely weak general reasoner can be told apart from one that got tanked by a single quirky task.

// bottom line

BBEH is a well-motivated, genuinely difficult successor to BBH that buys real headroom for now (9.8%/44.8% ceilings), and its self-flagged reference-model bias is more honesty than most benchmark papers offer. But that same adversarial-tuning process ties the benchmark's rankings to the idiosyncrasies of two specific Gemini checkpoints, roughly half its tasks are borrowed rather than newly built, and — as of this writing — an entire frontier model family (Claude) is untested against it. Treat BBEH's absolute numbers as a snapshot of early-2025 reasoning ceilings, not a fixed yardstick; the same saturation cycle that killed BBH will eventually come for BBEH's easier tasks too.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. BBEH replaces all 23 BBH tasks with harder analogues, calibrated so two Gemini reference models score below 70% on each — the best general-purpose model (Gemini 2.0 Flash) still only reaches 9.8% harmonic-mean accuracy; the best reasoning model (o3-mini, high) reaches 44.8%.
  2. The difficulty comes from more reasoning hops, ~6× longer inputs, and explicit shortcut-removal (e.g., blocking the "generate one line of Python" escape hatch on Boolean Expressions) — not from new skill domains. BBEH tests the same broad reasoning-skill families as BBH, just versions the old benchmark could no longer discriminate on.
  3. Reasoning-specialized models gain the most over general-purpose ones on counting, planning, arithmetic, and algorithmic tasks, and the least — sometimes negatively — on commonsense, humor, sarcasm, and causal-judgment tasks: general reasoning is not one dial, and test-time compute doesn't move every skill equally.
